This selection process is dynamic, requiring continuous refinement to ensure search accuracy and relevance. It is also important to acknowledge the need to balance comprehensiveness with precision. While broader keywords might capture a wider range of studies, they risk including irrelevant content. Conversely, overly specific terms could exclude valuable research. In this context, several key factors were considered when finalizing search string for this SLR. A thorough initial search of keywords was initiated with broader terms like "generative artificial intelligence" and "predictive maintenance" in the aviation domain. However, these terms yielded a vast number of results that lacked the specific focus desired. For instance, the search term "generative artificial intelligence" included studies on various deep learning and machine learning models, which wasn’t relevant to the specific research interest of thesis centered on generative AI models used for predicting aircraft maintenance needs. To address this challenge, a keyword refinement process was carried out. A preliminary review of existing literature was conducted, carefully examining the titles, abstracts, and keywords of relevant articles. This analysis helped identify synonyms, abbreviations, and related terms that could be incorporated into the search string. A crucial metric of predictive maintenance, "Remaining Useful Life," was added. Additionally, interchangeable terms related to Prognostics and Health Management, and "prognostics," were also added. Terms related to generative AI such as "generative," "adversarial," "GAN," "VAE," and "variational autoencoder" were included. Finally, aviation, aero, and aircraft were added to ensure the research focused on the aviation sector. domain_applicability.reason: Beschreibt primär reviewspezifische Such- und Einschlusskriterien statt übertragbare fachliche Methoden.

hypotheses.statement: Generative KI fungiert im Aircraft Predictive Maintenance primär als Daten-Enabler, indem sie synthetische Degradations-, Fehler- und Remaining-Useful-Life-Daten erzeugt, um den Mangel an Run-to-Failure-Daten in der Luftfahrt zu kompensieren.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die SLR beschreibt begrenzte Run-to-Failure-Daten als zentrales Luftfahrtproblem und positioniert generative KI explizit als Lösung durch Erzeugung synthetischer Daten; in der Schlussfolgerung wird die Generierung realistischer RUL-Daten als primäres Ziel genannt.

hypotheses.statement: Methodisch wird generative KI im Aircraft Health Monitoring vor allem für Datenaugmentation, Merkmalsextraktion und Imputation fehlender Sensordaten eingesetzt; dominant sind dabei GANs, VAEs und Hybridvarianten.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die SLR nennt diese drei Rollen ausdrücklich für RQ1 und identifiziert GANs, VAEs und Kombinationen als die am häufigsten verwendeten Modellklassen; die diskutierten Validierungen betreffen Sensor-, Teststand- und APU/Flugdatensätze.

hypotheses.statement: In der industriellen Umsetzung ist generative KI in der Flugzeuginstandhaltung noch eher emergent als breit operationalisiert, weil Wissensaustausch, Interpretierbarkeit, Systemspezifität und Echtzeitintegration die Überführung in bestehende MRO-/PdM-Prozesse begrenzen.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die SLR benennt Knowledge Sharing, Modellintegration und Spezifität als Kernherausforderungen und ergänzt Interpretierbarkeit sowie Realzeit-/Infrastrukturhürden; das MRO-Dokument betont zwar AI-getriebene Optimierung, formuliert diese aber als Transformations- und Umsetzungsagenda statt als breit erreichten Standard.

hypotheses.statement: Für sicherheits- und regulierungsnahe Luftfahrtkontexte ist generative KI derzeit eher als unterstützende Komponente innerhalb bestehender PHM/PdM- und wartungsplanerischer Prozesse einzuordnen als als autonomer Ersatz etablierter Verfahren.
hypotheses.reasoning: NASA beschreibt, dass PdM nicht für alle Systeme geeignet ist und bestehende MRB/MSG-Wartungsprogramme PdM-Vorteile bislang nicht integrieren; zugleich verweist die SLR auf Vertrauens- und Interpretierbarkeitsdefizite bei generativen Modellen, was ihre Rolle als ergänzende statt autonome Instanz stützt.

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: maintenance solutions, MRO providers must adapt to remain competitive and meet the diverse needs of airlines. By embracing digital transformation, offering scalable services, and fostering stronger collaboration with airline clients, MRO providers can position themselves as leaders in this evolving landscape. The following recommendations outline key strategies for achieving these goals. To stay competitive in a technology-driven industry, MRO providers should prioritize digital transformation by integrating advanced tools into their operations. Predictive maintenance tools powered by IoT-enabled sensors and real-time analytics can proactively identify potential issues, reducing unplanned downtime for airlines. Developing ATSaaS capabilities allows MRO providers to offer real-time, personalized maintenance solutions, making advanced services accessible to SMAs. Additionally, incorporating AI-driven tools enhances fault diagnosis, optimizes resource allocation, and supports more effective decision-making in maintenance processes. MRO providers can cater to a broader range of airline clients by designing modular maintenance packages. Customizable modules allow large airlines to address their unique fleet requirements, such as component-specific maintenance or fleet-wide overhauls. Stan- dardized personalization elements, such as real-time monitoring and dynamic scheduling, can make services more cost-effective and scalable for SMAs. Flexible pricing models, including subscription-based or pay-as-you-go options, further attract a diverse client base and accommodate fluctuating airline budgets. Collaboration with airlines is critical for understanding their maintenance needs and delivering effective solutions. MRO providers should engage in collaborative planning to develop maintenance strategies that combine the precision of customization with the adaptability of personalization tools. Creating integrated ecosystems that link airline data, maintenance schedules, and compliance tracking fosters seamless communication and coordination. Proactive communication with clients, including sharing predictive insights and recommendations, helps optimize maintenance strategies and strengthen relationships. Personalization depends on high-quality data and seamless integration with airline systems. MRO providers should develop centralized platforms that consolidate data from multiple sources, such as aircraft sensors, operational records, and airline feedback. Ensur- ing interoperability with existing airline infrastructure minimizes integration challenges, while robust cybersecurity measures protect sensitive operational and maintenance data. Equipping staff with the skills to manage advanced maintenance technologies is essential. MRO providers should offer technical training programs focused on using predictive analytics, IoT devices, and AI tools. Continuous learning opportunities ensure that technicians and engineers remain up to date with emerging trends, while customer- centric training enhances collaboration with airline clients to align services with their operational priorities. Sustainability is increasingly important in aviation, and MRO providers can play a key role by adopting environmentally friendly practices. Predictive tools can optimize resource use by minimizing

content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: questions. The disagreements were carefully reflected upon, and decisions were made accordingly. Finally, the study acknowledges limitations in its internal and external validity. The research questions themselves might have missed crucial aspects or made assumptions due to the evolving nature of the field and the vast number of approaches for developing these that technologies. Moreover, only considering studies combined GAI with predictive maintenance limited the findings' generalizability. This means they might not be applicable to other forms of predictive maintenance techniques. 6. Conclusion This study explored the growing use of Generative AI in transforming how the aviation industry performs predictive maintenance on aircraft. A systematic literature review was conducted, analyzing studies published over the past five years. The focus was on understanding how GAI is the role revolutionizing aircraft PdM. This generative models play, the specific models being used, the areas where GAI is applied, and the datasets employed to validate these models. included The study implemented a semi-automated approach for the literature review, utilizing ASReview and PRISMA techniques. This process identified 16 relevant studies out of a pool of 442. The findings highlight the significant contribution of GAI in surpassing traditional PdM methods. Notably, GAI excels at addressing limitations caused by a lack of data. This allows for more accurate diagnoses of equipment degradation and the ability to estimate the RUL of aircraft systems with greater precision. The primary objective of utilizing GAI in aircraft PdM is to generate realistic RUL data. This data is then used to predict RUL and diagnose faults. GAI proves particularly adept at tackling challenges like imbalanced and noisy data, information on normal operations significantly where outweighs data on faults. GANs, VAE-GANs, and various combinations of these models are the most prevalent GAI approaches used for predicting aircraft component RUL. the Across reviewed studies, NASA's CMAPSS emerged as the most popular dataset for validating GAI- based PdM models. However, a limitation of this dataset is its focus on turbofan engines. This restricts the possibilities for conducting research on PdM for other aircraft components. 7. References [1] Marais, K. B., & Robichaud, M. R. (2012). Analysis of trends in aviation maintenance risk: An empirical approach. Reliability Engineering & System Safety, 106, 104-118. content.abstract_or_snippet: statistics, and even artificial intelligence, this approach finds possible equipment problems before into serious malfunctions. However, the quantity and quality of the data gathered are crucial to the prediction's accuracy because inaccurate or insufficient data might provide erroneous models [12]. Finally, the hybrid approach combines these methods. The recent surge in machine data, combined with the power of deep learning, is significantly improving the accuracy of PdM models. This leads to more targeted the maintenance resources on strategies, focusing they turn components most likely to fail, ultimately leading to optimized maintenance plans [8]. As industrial demands for efficiency and reliability are pushing traditional maintenance methods to their limits, Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) has emerged as a powerful solution in aviation industry [10], in which PdM is also a key element [12]. By analyzing real-time and historical data, PHM can assess aircraft equipment health and predict future issues. Machine learning, a key tool within PHM, utilizes algorithms trained on vast amounts of data to predict equipment health or remaining useful life (RUL) [6]. However, a major hurdle lies in the need for labeled data (data with known outcomes) for training these algorithms [13]. Limited labeled data can significantly restrict the effectiveness of machine learning in PdM [14]. Acquiring the ideal data for machine learning in PdM for aircraft is practically challenging. This data, commonly known as "run-to-failure" data, consists of time-series readings that capture the gradual degradation of a component – from a healthy state all the way to complete failure [15]. The problem lies in the extended timelines involved. For instance, a seemingly minor issue in a critical aircraft part might take months or even years to progress to complete failure under normal operating conditions. However, allowing a component to reach this point is not only unacceptable but also poses a serious safety risk. To prioritize safety, aircraft parts are routinely replaced well before reaching a critical failure state. Researchers are actively addressing this challenge by developing generative AI models that can predict future equipment health even with scarce failure data [16]. In order to improve PdM procedures in the aviation sector and get beyond data constraints, generative AI (GAI) models are an effective technique. These algorithms may create synthetic data that imitates real-world "run-to-failure" circumstances after being trained on available data, even if it is incomplete. This synthetic data can then be used to train other machine learning algorithms for PdM tasks. Therefore, machine learning models may be able to forecast equipment

As aging planes with limited sensor capabilities are retired, a significant surge in maintenance requirements is inevitable, potentially overwhelming existing data management systems under the deluge of data from modern aircraft. This confluence of trends demands innovative solutions to ensure safe and efficient air travel in the coming decades. In the the aviation industry, various approaches to maintenance are practiced and adopted. The literature offers a variety of maintenance management approaches with diverse terminology. However, Adryan & Sastra [6] put together most common classification of these practices: Reactive approach is the most basic approach, which involves fixing problems only after they arise. Preventive approach on the other hand entails scheduling maintenance tasks based on equipment operating hours or predetermined time intervals. Predictive approach, which is the focus of this study, utilizes data analysis and monitoring techniques to predict potential equipment failures before they happen. These maintenance strategies determine types of maintenance activities and their timing to be undertaken in the decision-making process. Nonetheless, because of the growing volume of data being gathered by the onboard sensors of contemporary aircraft, the aviation sector is aggressively utilizing predictive maintenance, or PdM [7]. The analysis of data from a machine's performance, condition, and other health indicators is the core goal of predictive maintenance. By optimizing the time between repairs, this technique assists in determining the best time for maintenance. Unlike traditional maintenance schedules, PdM uses sensor networks to monitor the machine and trigger maintenance actions only when pre-defined conditions indicate a potential failure [8]. An International Air Transport Association (IATA) report from 2015 suggests that predictive maintenance could potentially increase aircraft availability by up to 35% [9]. This increased availability directly overall translates maintenance costs. reduced to Researchers have identified three main approaches to predictive maintenance: physical modeling, data-driven, and hybrid [10]. Physics-based modelling uses existing knowledge of a system to create mathematical models that predict how it will degrade over time [11]. However, time-consuming and building such models can be computationally expensive, especially for complex systems with numerous interacting components [10]. In contrast, data-driven approach focuses on monitoring a system's condition and learning from historical data to predict its future state. Utilizing reliability functions, statistics, and even artificial intelligence, this approach finds possible equipment problems before into serious malfunctions. However, the quantity and quality of the data gathered are crucial to the prediction's accuracy because inaccurate or insufficient data might provide erroneous models [12]. Finally,

Abstract Predictive maintenance (PdM) is a critical tool in aviation, promoting sustainability, safety, and cost-effectiveness. However, a significant challenge in implementing predictive maintenance framework is limited run-to-failure data due to frequent preventive maintenance. Generative AI (GAI) offers a promising solution to this challenge by generating synthetic data, enabling more accurate predictions of aircraft system health. The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. This research addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review (SLR), employing an active learning open-source tool to thoroughly

In essence, existing systematic reviews offer valuable insights into PdM using various techniques, but they lack a dedicated focus on generative AI models within the specific context of aviation predictive maintenance. This gap presents a compelling opportunity for further research to explore the potential and unique functionalities of generative AI in enhancing predictive maintenance practices for the aviation industry. First, it strictly followed a well- established method called SLR protocol. Second, it narrowed the focus to the specific combination of generative AI and predictive maintenance within the aviation industry. By focusing on a specific niche and employing a systematic approach, this study delivers unique and valuable insights. 1.2 Paper Organization This paper offers a comprehensive review of GAI based PdM in aviation industry, following a structured approach. Chapter 2 outlines the SLR protocol in detail, which combines both manual and semi-automated approaches. Chapter 3 describes the findings of the SLR. It begins by presenting key statistics of the SLR sample, followed by comprehensive exploration of each research question. Chapter 4 dives into a discussion. To achieve this goal effectively, a structured methodology is crucial. Kitchenham & Charters [19] breaks down the process of conducting a systematic review into three key phases: Planning, conducting and reporting of reviews. The entire review protocol of this research has therefore been constructed on guidelines of Kitchenham & Charters [19] and Van Dinter et al. [20]. Figure 1 represents the review protocol for this study. 2.1 Research Questions This study aims to find all relevant information on applying generative AI for predictive maintenance in the aviation industry. To effectively integrate generative AI, three key areas require investigation: (1) its purpose and extent of its utilization, (2) development approaches for generative AI-based predictive maintenance algorithms, and (3) anticipated challenges in implementation. To address these areas, research questions were formulated: following the • RQ1: What is the role of Generative AI in aircraft PdM? • RQ2: Which Generative models are used in Aircraft PdM? • RQ3: Which application domains exist in Generative AI based Aircraft PdM? • RQ4: Which datasets are used to validate Generative AI based models in aircraft PdM? While the dotted line depicts a slow and steady increase in relevant studies found through a random manual search, the solid line representing the active learning process demonstrates a much steeper rise. This signifies that the active learning method identifies relevant studies at a significantly faster rate. 3.3 RQ-1: What is the role of Generative AI in Aircraft PdM? Aircraft rely on healthy systems for safe and efficient operations, and it is identified that predictive maintenance based on GAI plays a crucial role in capturing the faults before they even occur. Generative AI offers a powerful suite of techniques to address the challenges of imbalanced and scarce data for capturing the early stages of aircraft system degradation. Hence, the primary roles of generative AI are s d r o c e r t n a v e e r l f o r e b m u N 55 46 37 28 18 9 0 1 Active Learning Manual Learning 25 49 74 98 122 Number of reviewed records Fig. 8 Active Learning Vs Manual Learning (1) data augmentation, (2) feature extraction and (3) missing data imputation. Data augmentation refers to generation of new data and augmenting it with existing data. The goal of data augmentation is to increase data quantity and diversity [27]. Moreover, GAI can be used to extract key features of the data for building classifiers and predictive models, hence assisting in learning data representation [28]. Finally, data imputation techniques involve filling in the blanks in the data. Unlike traditional estimation techniques of statistical analysis, neural network methods work in two stages. First, they train a model on the data. Then, in the imputation stage, the trained model is used to "fill in the blanks" for missing values by generating data that follows those learned patterns [29]. Aircraft generate a lot of data through sensors installed but the key problem in implementing PdM is that the data is imbalanced. This means that normal operation readings significantly outnumber anomaly data. This imbalance mirrors situations where the number of healthy samples vastly outweighs defective ones, or the training data fails to represent all fault categories equally. Consequently, GAI models can become biased towards the over-represented normal class, leading to misleading performance metrics like accuracy. To address this challenge, various techniques are being used to balance the data before training the models. This involved oversampling the rare "failure" data or under sampling the abundant "normal" data. Wu et al. [30],

Limitations of Research it and testing refinement; however, Although the search protocol was formulated after thorough is acknowledged that the search for relevant papers might have excluded some valuable studies. Phrasing search queries can be tricky, and even minor wording changes can significantly alter search results in different databases. Furthermore, the search of relevant studies was concluded in Feb 2024, excluding any research published afterwards. This is a challenge in a rapidly developing field like generative AI. With so many new publications, capturing all relevant literature up to the review's submission date is difficult. Even with efficient research methods, there's always a time gap between data collection and the final review. This means there might be recently published techniques that address the issues identified in this review, but they weren't included due to the cutoff date. This can potentially lead to missing important research on the topic. Another limitation is publication bias. Studies with positive results are more likely to be published than those with negative findings. The authors tried to address this by focusing on high-quality research using specific methods, but there's still a chance relevant information was missed. Selection bias is also a concern. Even with predetermined criteria for including studies, subjective judgments can still influence which ones the research area, are chosen. The broad scope of encompassing both predictive maintenance and generative AI, requires expertise in various fields. This increases the possibility of misinterpreting some studies during the selection process. The data extraction and synthesis process were equally shared between the authors to capture important information needed to answer the research questions. The disagreements were carefully reflected upon, and decisions were made accordingly. Finally, the study acknowledges limitations in its internal and external validity. content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: . The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. This research addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review (SLR), employing an active learning open-source tool to thoroughly analyze papers sourced from four scientific databases. These papers focus on predictive maintenance of aircraft systems through generative AI models. The findings of this review examine various aspects of GAI-powered predictive maintenance, including its objectives, the diversity of models employed, areas of application, datasets used for model validation, and the prevailing challenges and emerging trends. The review identified that most commonly used GAI models in aircraft PdM are GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks), VAEs (Variational Autoencoders) or combination of these models. However, knowledge sharing, model integration and specificity are the key challenges associated with the implementation of these models. This strong knowledge -base will be a valuable resource for researchers, engineers, and practitioners aiming to improve their knowledge and implementation of GAI- driven models for predictive maintenance in the aviation industry. Keywords: Generative AI, Predictive Maintenance (PdM), Aircraft Maintenance, Generative Adversarial Network, Data Augmentation 1. Introduction Maintenance plays a pivotal role in the aviation industry, with its importance deeply rooted in ensuring the reliability and safety of aircraft operations. Operating in an environment characterized by extreme temperatures, high altitudes, and various forces during flight, aircraft systems undergo age-related degradation and operational wear without regular maintenance. The repercussions of such neglect are severe, leading to system failures that can compromise the safety of passengers and crew on board [1]. Conversely, the financial ramifications of grounded aircraft due to insufficient maintenance are substantial, compelling airlines to prioritize comprehensive maintenance programs [2]. Regular maintenance actions not only strengthen the structural and operational integrity of aircraft but also extend the lifespan of critical equipment, mitigating revenue losses associated with unplanned downtime [3]. According to a report by Statista, the financial burden of unscheduled aircraft maintenance is anticipated to skyrocket in the aviation industry, reaching an estimated annual cost of nearly 39 billion USD by 2035 [4]. This projection is particularly alarming considering the expected surge in the global fleet size, forecasted to almost double from 25,900 to 47,080 aircraft between 2019 and 2041, due to increased demand for both passenger and cargo transportation [5]. As aging planes with limited sensor capabilities are retired, a significant surge in maintenance requirements is inevitable, potentially overwhelming existing data management systems

VAEs, on the other hand, are being utilized to extract meaningful and information features from the data. All the proposed approaches, combining under-sampling, feature space synthetic data generation, aimed to create a balanced training set with superior synthetic anomalies, ultimately enhancing anomaly detection accuracy in aircraft equipment. Nevertheless, few challenges remain in implementation of GAI models. One of the challenges identified is optimization of the balance between generator and discriminator for realistic synthetic data [30,40]. The current research heavily focuses on sensor data, ignoring the fact that textual and visual data can also augment GAI models. Furthermore, lack of interpretability causes lack of trust in the output of GAI models especially for safety critical systems, as a lesser understanding generally exists into how these models arrive at their conclusion. Also, GAI models trained on specific aircraft system might not work well with other systems, due to sensor data variations. Finally, the implementation of generative models in PdM of aircraft is affected by high-dimensional aircraft data, expensive real-time integration challenges. The successful integration of GAI model in PdM framework depends upon the knowledge dissemination. The lack of knowledge sharing may result in a breakdown in communication and make it difficult to incorporate generative AI models into current operations [27,30,35]. infrastructure, and turbofan engine It was observed that public repositories offer a wealth of data, with the C-MAPSS emerging as the dominant choice for [36,38]. This dataset research encompasses information on 100 engines including run-to- failure data, facilitating RUL prediction studies. However, C-MAPSS focuses solely on turbofan engines, potentially limiting research on other critical aircraft components like bearings and hydraulics. Despite these limitations, there exists positive prospects for research in this field. Researchers are trying to explore various strategies to collect more data through simulation in real-world scenarios. Research techniques or collaboration with airlines. This allows the researchers to assess how well their fault diagnosis method performs in identifying issues within these high-speed bearings under diverse operating conditions [31]. Other experimental datasets found in the literature were based on test rigs and practical settings. Fu et al. [34] used ground-based test rig simulating an aviation hydraulic pump to collect vibration signals during fault simulations. Similarly, Wu et al. [30] investigated bearing faults using a rotating machinery platform. Duan et al. [40] validated their method for estimating remaining useful life in a practical setting using real-world sensor readings from the APU on an Airbus A320 aircraft during its environmental control mode. Likewise, Huang et al. [39] investigated real-world airplane data to detect malfunctions. They collected comprehensive flight data from 47 sensors on each aircraft throughout the entire flight (takeoff to landing). This data includes information on both normal flights and those experiencing two specific types of problems. 4. Discussion This study is the first comprehensive SLR to explore how Generative AI can be used for predicting maintenance needs in aircraft. We looked at over 400 research papers from 2019 and narrowed it down to 16 high-quality studies. An active learning process was implemented using ASReview Lab to select relevant articles based on abstracts. The process was stopped after the appearance of 22 consecutive irrelevant articles. The selection process could have been stopped earlier but that would have slightly increased the risk of losing relevant articles. learning, and attention-guided Notably, all papers clearly stated the objectives of addressing the challenge of limited run-to-failure data of through generative models. Data scarcity, imbalanced dataset, noisy and missing data are some key challenges posed to accurate predictions of RUL and fault diagnosis [28,29,30,32,35,37,41,42]. The most popular models employed for GAI based PdM are GANs and VAEs. Different variants of GANs, such as CGANs, DGANs, WGANs, RNN-GANs, CR-GANs etc. are being adopted by researchers to address the issues of limited data. However, the choice of the specific GAN variant is dependent on the nature of the data and the desired level of interpretability. VAEs, on the other hand, are being utilized to extract meaningful and information features from the data. All the proposed approaches, combining under-sampling, feature space synthetic data

content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: ACM Digital Library • IEEE Xplore • Emerald • Research Articles • Review Articles • Conference Proceedings Language • English Table 1: Review scope of this study CONDUCTING REPORTING a diverse range of reputable academic databases was selected, such as ScienceDirect, IEEE Xplore, etc. Moreover, only English language publications with a strong reputation (cite score or impact factor > 1) were included to ensure credibility and avoid bias. The details of this search strategy are summarized in Table 1. 2.3 Search String Identifying high-quality research articles depends on selecting the right keywords. This selection process is dynamic, requiring continuous refinement to ensure search accuracy and relevance. It is also important to acknowledge the need to balance comprehensiveness with precision. While broader keywords might capture a wider range of studies, they risk including irrelevant content. Conversely, overly specific terms could exclude valuable research. In this context, several key factors were considered when finalizing search string for this SLR. A thorough initial search of keywords was initiated with broader terms like "generative artificial intelligence" and "predictive maintenance" in the aviation domain. However, these terms yielded a vast number of results that lacked the specific focus desired. For instance, the search term "generative artificial intelligence" included studies on various deep learning and machine learning models, which wasn’t relevant to the specific research interest of thesis centered on generative AI models used for predicting aircraft maintenance needs. To address this challenge, a keyword refinement process was carried out. A preliminary review of existing literature was conducted, carefully examining the titles, abstracts, and keywords of relevant articles. This analysis helped identify synonyms, abbreviations, and related terms that could be incorporated into the search string. A crucial metric of predictive maintenance, "Remaining Useful Life," was added. Additionally, interchangeable terms related to Prognostics and Health Management, and "prognostics," were also added. Terms related to generative AI such as "generative," "adversarial," "GAN," "VAE," and "variational autoencoder" were included. Finally, aviation, aero, and aircraft were added to ensure the research focused on the aviation sector. domain_applicability.reason: Beschreibt primär reviewspezifische Such- und Einschlusskriterien statt übertragbare fachliche Methoden.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Generative KI fungiert im Aircraft Predictive Maintenance primär als Daten-Enabler, indem sie synthetische Degradations-, Fehler- und Remaining-Useful-Life-Daten erzeugt, um den Mangel an Run-to-Failure-Daten in der Luftfahrt zu kompensieren.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Sehr gute Passung zur Evidenz: Die SLR beschreibt den Mangel an gelabelten bzw. Run-to-Failure-Daten in der Luftfahrt als zentrales Problem und stellt generative KI explizit als Mittel zur Erzeugung synthetischer Degradations-, Fehler- und insbesondere realistischer RUL-Daten dar. Die Hypothese ist spezifisch und wird durch mehrere Stellen derselben SLR konsistent gestützt; belastbare Gegenbelege liegen in den bereitgestellten Dokumenten nicht vor.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Methodisch wird generative KI im Aircraft Health Monitoring vor allem für Datenaugmentation, Merkmalsextraktion und Imputation fehlender Sensordaten eingesetzt; dominant sind dabei GANs, VAEs und Hybridvarianten.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Hohe semantische Passung: Die SLR beantwortet RQ1/RQ2 direkt und nennt Datenaugmentation, Merkmalsextraktion und Imputation fehlender Daten als Primärrollen generativer KI; zugleich werden GANs, VAEs und Kombinationen dieser Modelle als dominierende Klassen ausgewiesen. Die Aussage wird zusätzlich durch Hinweise auf verschiedene Validierungsdatensätze und Anwendungskontexte gestützt. Gegenbelege fehlen.

updated_hypotheses.statement: In der industriellen Umsetzung ist generative KI in der Flugzeuginstandhaltung noch eher emergent als breit operationalisiert, weil Wissensaustausch, Interpretierbarkeit, Systemspezifität und Echtzeitintegration die Überführung in bestehende MRO-/PdM-Prozesse begrenzen.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Hypothese passt insgesamt gut zu den dokumentierten Implementierungsbarrieren: Die SLR nennt Knowledge Sharing, Interpretierbarkeit, Systemspezifität und Echtzeit-/Infrastrukturprobleme als wesentliche Hürden; das MRO-Dokument formuliert AI-getriebene Optimierung überwiegend als Transformations- und Entwicklungsagenda. Allerdings ist die Aussage über den Industrialisierungsgrad ('noch eher emergent als breit operationalisiert') stärker als die direkte Evidenzbasis, die eher Barrieren und Empfehlungen als belastbare Adoptionsraten liefert. Daher derzeit gut plausibel, aber noch nicht vollständig validiert.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Für sicherheits- und regulierungsnahe Luftfahrtkontexte ist generative KI derzeit eher als unterstützende Komponente innerhalb bestehender PHM/PdM- und wartungsplanerischer Prozesse einzuordnen als als autonomer Ersatz etablierter Verfahren.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Evidenz stützt die Einordnung generativer KI als unterstützende Komponente eher als autonomen Ersatz: NASA betont, dass PdM nicht für alle Systeme geeignet ist und in bestehende Wartungsprogramme eingebettet werden muss; die SLR verweist zusätzlich auf Vertrauens-, Interpretierbarkeits- und Integrationsprobleme. Allerdings beziehen sich die NASA-Dokumente auf PdM allgemein und nicht spezifisch auf generative KI, sodass die Schlussfolgerung für generative KI im sicherheits- und regulierungsnahen Luftfahrtkontext plausibel, aber nur indirekt gestützt ist.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

challenge_findings.description: Die bereitgestellte SLR identifiziert Datenknappheit bzw. fehlende Run-to-Failure-Daten als Kernproblem und beschreibt die Generierung realistischer synthetischer RUL-/Degradationsdaten als zentrale Rolle generativer KI im Aircraft PdM.

Die SLR benennt Datenaugmentation, Feature Extraction und Missing-Data-Imputation explizit als Primärfunktionen generativer KI und verweist auf GANs, VAEs sowie Hybridformen als die am häufigsten eingesetzten Modellklassen.

Die Evidenz dokumentiert zwar zentrale Umsetzungsbarrieren, quantifiziert aber nicht belastbar, wie weit generative KI in realen MRO-/PdM-Prozessen bereits operationalisiert ist. Für die starke Aussage über einen nur emergenten Industrialisierungsgrad wäre breitere Evidenz zur tatsächlichen Adoption nötig.

Die Schlussfolgerung, dass generative KI vor allem unterstützend und nicht autonom eingesetzt wird, ist plausibel, stützt sich jedoch teilweise auf allgemeine PdM-Barrieren und Eignungsgrenzen statt auf direkte Evidenz zu generativer KI in regulierten Luftfahrtprozessen.
Fit score below threshold; broaden supporting evidence base.

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: maintenance solutions, MRO providers must adapt to remain competitive and meet the diverse needs of airlines. By embracing digital transformation, offering scalable services, and fostering stronger collaboration with airline clients, MRO providers can position themselves as leaders in this evolving landscape. The following recommendations outline key strategies for achieving these goals. To stay competitive in a technology-driven industry, MRO providers should prioritize digital transformation by integrating advanced tools into their operations. Predictive maintenance tools powered by IoT-enabled sensors and real-time analytics can proactively identify potential issues, reducing unplanned downtime for airlines. Developing ATSaaS capabilities allows MRO providers to offer real-time, personalized maintenance solutions, making advanced services accessible to SMAs. Additionally, incorporating AI-driven tools enhances fault diagnosis, optimizes resource allocation, and supports more effective decision-making in maintenance processes. MRO providers can cater to a broader range of airline clients by designing modular maintenance packages. Customizable modules allow large airlines to address their unique fleet requirements, such as component-specific maintenance or fleet-wide overhauls. Stan- dardized personalization elements, such as real-time monitoring and dynamic scheduling, can make services more cost-effective and scalable for SMAs. Flexible pricing models, including subscription-based or pay-as-you-go options, further attract a diverse client base and accommodate fluctuating airline budgets. Collaboration with airlines is critical for understanding their maintenance needs and delivering effective solutions. MRO providers should engage in collaborative planning to develop maintenance strategies that combine the precision of customization with the adaptability of personalization tools. Creating integrated ecosystems that link airline data, maintenance schedules, and compliance tracking fosters seamless communication and coordination. Proactive communication with clients, including sharing predictive insights and recommendations, helps optimize maintenance strategies and strengthen relationships. Personalization depends on high-quality data and seamless integration with airline systems. MRO providers should develop centralized platforms that consolidate data from multiple sources, such as aircraft sensors, operational records, and airline feedback. Ensur- ing interoperability with existing airline infrastructure minimizes integration challenges, while robust cybersecurity measures protect sensitive operational and maintenance data. Equipping staff with the skills to manage advanced maintenance technologies is essential. MRO providers should offer technical training programs focused on using predictive analytics, IoT devices, and AI tools. Continuous learning opportunities ensure that technicians and engineers remain up to date with emerging trends, while customer- centric training enhances collaboration with airline clients to align services with their operational priorities. Sustainability is increasingly important in aviation, and MRO providers can play a key role by adopting environmentally friendly practices. Predictive tools can optimize resource use by minimizing

Customizable modules allow large airlines to address their unique fleet requirements, such as component-specific maintenance or fleet-wide overhauls. Stan- dardized personalization elements, such as real-time monitoring and dynamic scheduling, can make services more cost-effective and scalable for SMAs. Flexible pricing models, including subscription-based or pay-as-you-go options, further attract a diverse client base and accommodate fluctuating airline budgets. Collaboration with airlines is critical for understanding their maintenance needs and delivering effective solutions. MRO providers should engage in collaborative planning to develop maintenance strategies that combine the precision of customization with the adaptability of personalization tools. Creating integrated ecosystems that link airline data, maintenance schedules, and compliance tracking fosters seamless communication and coordination. Proactive communication with clients, including sharing predictive insights and recommendations, helps optimize maintenance strategies and strengthen relationships. Personalization depends on high-quality data and seamless integration with airline systems. MRO providers should develop centralized platforms that consolidate data from multiple sources, such as aircraft sensors, operational records, and airline feedback. Ensur- ing interoperability with existing airline infrastructure minimizes integration challenges, while robust cybersecurity measures protect sensitive operational and maintenance data. Equipping staff with the skills to manage advanced maintenance technologies is essential. MRO providers should offer technical training programs focused on using predictive analytics, IoT devices, and AI tools. Continuous learning opportunities ensure that technicians and engineers remain up to date with emerging trends, while customer- centric training enhances collaboration with airline clients to align services with their operational priorities. Conclusion This study explored the growing use of Generative AI in transforming how the aviation industry performs predictive maintenance on aircraft. A systematic literature review was conducted, analyzing studies published over the past five years. The focus was on understanding how GAI is the role revolutionizing aircraft PdM. This generative models play, the specific models being used, the areas where GAI is applied, and the datasets employed to validate these models. included The study implemented a semi-automated approach for the literature review, utilizing ASReview and PRISMA techniques. This process identified 16 relevant studies out of a pool of 442. The findings highlight the significant contribution of GAI in surpassing traditional PdM methods. Notably, GAI excels at addressing limitations caused by a lack of data. This allows for more accurate diagnoses of equipment degradation and the ability to estimate the RUL of aircraft systems with greater precision. The primary objective of utilizing GAI in aircraft PdM is to generate realistic RUL data. This data is then used to predict RUL and diagnose faults. GAI proves particularly adept at tackling challenges like imbalanced and noisy data, information on normal operations significantly where outweighs data on faults. GANs, VAE-GANs, and various combinations of these models are the most prevalent GAI approaches used for predicting aircraft component RUL. the Across reviewed studies, NASA's CMAPSS emerged as the most popular dataset for validating GAI- based PdM models. However, a limitation of this dataset is its focus on turbofan engines. This restricts the possibilities for conducting research on PdM for other aircraft components. 7. References [1] Marais, K. This leads to more targeted the maintenance resources on strategies, focusing they turn components most likely to fail, ultimately leading to optimized maintenance plans [8]. As industrial demands for efficiency and reliability are pushing traditional maintenance methods to their limits, Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) has emerged as a powerful solution in aviation industry [10], in which PdM is also a key element [12]. By analyzing real-time and historical data, PHM can assess aircraft equipment health and predict future issues. Machine learning, a key tool within PHM, utilizes algorithms trained on vast amounts of data to predict equipment health or remaining useful life (RUL) [6]. However, a major hurdle lies in the need for labeled data (data with known outcomes) for training these algorithms [13]. Limited labeled data can significantly restrict the effectiveness of machine learning in PdM [14]. Acquiring the ideal data for machine learning in PdM for aircraft is practically challenging. This data, commonly known as "run-to-failure" data, consists of time-series readings that capture the gradual degradation of a component – from a healthy state all the way to complete failure [15]. The problem lies in the extended timelines involved. For instance, a seemingly minor issue in a critical aircraft part might take months or even years to progress to complete failure under normal operating conditions. However, allowing a component to reach this point is not only unacceptable but also poses a serious safety risk. To prioritize safety, aircraft parts are routinely replaced well before reaching a critical failure state. Researchers are actively addressing this challenge by developing generative AI models that can predict future equipment health even with scarce failure data [16]. In order to improve PdM procedures in the aviation sector and get beyond data constraints, generative AI (GAI) models are an effective technique. These algorithms may create synthetic data that imitates real-world "run-to-failure" circumstances after being trained on available data, even if it is incomplete. This synthetic data can then be used to train other machine learning algorithms for PdM tasks. Therefore, machine learning models may be able to forecast equipment

content.abstract_or_snippet: ,080 aircraft between 2019 and 2041, due to increased demand for both passenger and cargo transportation [5]. As aging planes with limited sensor capabilities are retired, a significant surge in maintenance requirements is inevitable, potentially overwhelming existing data management systems under the deluge of data from modern aircraft. This confluence of trends demands innovative solutions to ensure safe and efficient air travel in the coming decades. In the the aviation industry, various approaches to maintenance are practiced and adopted. The literature offers a variety of maintenance management approaches with diverse terminology. However, Adryan & Sastra [6] put together most common classification of these practices: Reactive approach is the most basic approach, which involves fixing problems only after they arise. Preventive approach on the other hand entails scheduling maintenance tasks based on equipment operating hours or predetermined time intervals. Predictive approach, which is the focus of this study, utilizes data analysis and monitoring techniques to predict potential equipment failures before they happen. These maintenance strategies determine types of maintenance activities and their timing to be undertaken in the decision-making process. Nonetheless, because of the growing volume of data being gathered by the onboard sensors of contemporary aircraft, the aviation sector is aggressively utilizing predictive maintenance, or PdM [7]. The analysis of data from a machine's performance, condition, and other health indicators is the core goal of predictive maintenance. By optimizing the time between repairs, this technique assists in determining the best time for maintenance. Unlike traditional maintenance schedules, PdM uses sensor networks to monitor the machine and trigger maintenance actions only when pre-defined conditions indicate a potential failure [8]. An International Air Transport Association (IATA) report from 2015 suggests that predictive maintenance could potentially increase aircraft availability by up to 35% [9]. This increased availability directly overall translates maintenance costs. reduced to Researchers have identified three main approaches to predictive maintenance: physical modeling, data-driven, and hybrid [10]. Physics-based modelling uses existing knowledge of a system to create mathematical models that predict how it will degrade over time [11]. However, time-consuming and building such models can be computationally expensive, especially for complex systems with numerous interacting components [10]. In contrast, data-driven approach focuses on monitoring a system's condition and learning from historical data to predict its future state. Utilizing reliability functions, statistics, and even artificial intelligence, this approach finds possible equipment problems before into serious malfunctions. However, the quantity and quality of the data gathered are crucial to the prediction's accuracy because inaccurate or insufficient data might provide erroneous models [12]. Finally,

Abstract Predictive maintenance (PdM) is a critical tool in aviation, promoting sustainability, safety, and cost-effectiveness. However, a significant challenge in implementing predictive maintenance framework is limited run-to-failure data due to frequent preventive maintenance. Generative AI (GAI) offers a promising solution to this challenge by generating synthetic data, enabling more accurate predictions of aircraft system health. The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. In essence, existing systematic reviews offer valuable insights into PdM using various techniques, but they lack a dedicated focus on generative AI models within the specific context of aviation predictive maintenance. This gap presents a compelling opportunity for further research to explore the potential and unique functionalities of generative AI in enhancing predictive maintenance practices for the aviation industry. Keeping in view the above stated facts, to the best of author’s knowledge, this study stands out from previous research in two ways. First, it strictly followed a well- established method called SLR protocol. Second, it narrowed the focus to the specific combination of generative AI and predictive maintenance within the aviation industry. By focusing on a specific niche and employing a systematic approach, this study delivers unique and valuable insights. 1.2 Paper Organization This paper offers a comprehensive review of GAI based PdM in aviation industry, following a structured approach. Chapter 2 outlines the SLR protocol in detail, which combines both manual and semi-automated approaches. Chapter 3 describes the findings of the SLR. It begins by presenting key statistics of the SLR sample, followed by comprehensive exploration of each research question. Chapter 4 dives into a discussion. Subsequently, in chapter 6 the significance of this research is presented and concluding remarks are summarized. 2. content.abstract_or_snippet: literature review is to thoroughly examine existing research to uncover research gaps, trends, and other valuable insights. To achieve this goal effectively, a structured methodology is crucial. Kitchenham & Charters [19] breaks down the process of conducting a systematic review into three key phases: Planning, conducting and reporting of reviews. The entire review protocol of this research has therefore been constructed on guidelines of Kitchenham & Charters [19] and Van Dinter et al. [20]. Figure 1 represents the review protocol for this study. 2.1 Research Questions This study aims to find all relevant information on applying generative AI for predictive maintenance in the aviation industry. To effectively integrate generative AI, three key areas require investigation: (1) its purpose and extent of its utilization, (2) development approaches for generative AI-based predictive maintenance algorithms, and (3) anticipated challenges in implementation. To address these areas, research questions were formulated: following the • RQ1: What is the role of Generative AI in aircraft PdM? • RQ2: Which Generative models are used in Aircraft PdM? • RQ3: Which application domains exist in Generative AI based Aircraft PdM? • RQ4: Which datasets are used to validate Generative AI based models in aircraft PdM? content.abstract_or_snippet: for the active learning approach. While the dotted line depicts a slow and steady increase in relevant studies found through a random manual search, the solid line representing the active learning process demonstrates a much steeper rise. This signifies that the active learning method identifies relevant studies at a significantly faster rate. 3.3 RQ-1: What is the role of Generative AI in Aircraft PdM? Aircraft rely on healthy systems for safe and efficient operations, and it is identified that predictive maintenance based on GAI plays a crucial role in capturing the faults before they even occur. Generative AI offers a powerful suite of techniques to address the challenges of imbalanced and scarce data for capturing the early stages of aircraft system degradation. Hence, the primary roles of generative AI are s d r o c e r t n a v e e r l f o r e b m u N 55 46 37 28 18 9 0 1 Active Learning Manual Learning 25 49 74 98 122 Number of reviewed records Fig. 8 Active Learning Vs Manual Learning (1) data augmentation, (2) feature extraction and (3) missing data imputation. Data augmentation refers to generation of new data and augmenting it with existing data. The goal of data augmentation is to increase data quantity and diversity [27]. Moreover, GAI can be used to extract key features of the data for building classifiers and predictive models, hence assisting in learning data representation [28]. Finally, data imputation techniques involve filling in the blanks in the data. Unlike traditional estimation techniques of statistical analysis, neural network methods work in two stages. First, they train a model on the data. Then, in the imputation stage, the trained model is used to "fill in the blanks" for missing values by generating data that follows those learned patterns [29]. Aircraft generate a lot of data through sensors installed but the key problem in implementing PdM is that the data is imbalanced. This means that normal operation readings significantly outnumber anomaly data. This imbalance mirrors situations where the number of healthy samples vastly outweighs defective ones, or the training data fails to represent all fault categories equally. Consequently, GAI models can become biased towards the over-represented normal class, leading to misleading performance metrics like accuracy. To address this challenge, various techniques are being used to balance the data before training the models. This involved oversampling the rare "failure" data or under sampling the abundant "normal" data. Wu et al. [30],

Limitations of Research it and testing refinement; however, Although the search protocol was formulated after thorough is acknowledged that the search for relevant papers might have excluded some valuable studies. Phrasing search queries can be tricky, and even minor wording changes can significantly alter search results in different databases. Furthermore, the search of relevant studies was concluded in Feb 2024, excluding any research published afterwards. This is a challenge in a rapidly developing field like generative AI. With so many new publications, capturing all relevant literature up to the review's submission date is difficult. Even with efficient research methods, there's always a time gap between data collection and the final review. This means there might be recently published techniques that address the issues identified in this review, but they weren't included due to the cutoff date. This can potentially lead to missing important research on the topic. Another limitation is publication bias. Studies with positive results are more likely to be published than those with negative findings. The authors tried to address this by focusing on high-quality research using specific methods, but there's still a chance relevant information was missed. Selection bias is also a concern. Even with predetermined criteria for including studies, subjective judgments can still influence which ones the research area, are chosen. The broad scope of encompassing both predictive maintenance and generative AI, requires expertise in various fields. This increases the possibility of misinterpreting some studies during the selection process. The data extraction and synthesis process were equally shared between the authors to capture important information needed to answer the research questions. The disagreements were carefully reflected upon, and decisions were made accordingly. Finally, the study acknowledges limitations in its internal and external validity. content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: . The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. This research addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review (SLR), employing an active learning open-source tool to thoroughly analyze papers sourced from four scientific databases. These papers focus on predictive maintenance of aircraft systems through generative AI models. The findings of this review examine various aspects of GAI-powered predictive maintenance, including its objectives, the diversity of models employed, areas of application, datasets used for model validation, and the prevailing challenges and emerging trends. The review identified that most commonly used GAI models in aircraft PdM are GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks), VAEs (Variational Autoencoders) or combination of these models. However, knowledge sharing, model integration and specificity are the key challenges associated with the implementation of these models. This strong knowledge -base will be a valuable resource for researchers, engineers, and practitioners aiming to improve their knowledge and implementation of GAI- driven models for predictive maintenance in the aviation industry. Keywords: Generative AI, Predictive Maintenance (PdM), Aircraft Maintenance, Generative Adversarial Network, Data Augmentation 1. Introduction Maintenance plays a pivotal role in the aviation industry, with its importance deeply rooted in ensuring the reliability and safety of aircraft operations. Operating in an environment characterized by extreme temperatures, high altitudes, and various forces during flight, aircraft systems undergo age-related degradation and operational wear without regular maintenance. The repercussions of such neglect are severe, leading to system failures that can compromise the safety of passengers and crew on board [1]. Conversely, the financial ramifications of grounded aircraft due to insufficient maintenance are substantial, compelling airlines to prioritize comprehensive maintenance programs [2]. Regular maintenance actions not only strengthen the structural and operational integrity of aircraft but also extend the lifespan of critical equipment, mitigating revenue losses associated with unplanned downtime [3]. According to a report by Statista, the financial burden of unscheduled aircraft maintenance is anticipated to skyrocket in the aviation industry, reaching an estimated annual cost of nearly 39 billion USD by 2035 [4]. This projection is particularly alarming considering the expected surge in the global fleet size, forecasted to almost double from 25,900 to 47,080 aircraft between 2019 and 2041, due to increased demand for both passenger and cargo transportation [5]. As aging planes with limited sensor capabilities are retired, a significant surge in maintenance requirements is inevitable, potentially overwhelming existing data management systems

This selection process is dynamic, requiring continuous refinement to ensure search accuracy and relevance. It is also important to acknowledge the need to balance comprehensiveness with precision. While broader keywords might capture a wider range of studies, they risk including irrelevant content. Conversely, overly specific terms could exclude valuable research. In this context, several key factors were considered when finalizing search string for this SLR. A thorough initial search of keywords was initiated with broader terms like "generative artificial intelligence" and "predictive maintenance" in the aviation domain. However, these terms yielded a vast number of results that lacked the specific focus desired. For instance, the search term "generative artificial intelligence" included studies on various deep learning and machine learning models, which wasn’t relevant to the specific research interest of thesis centered on generative AI models used for predicting aircraft maintenance needs. To address this challenge, a keyword refinement process was carried out. A preliminary review of existing literature was conducted, carefully examining the titles, abstracts, and keywords of relevant articles. This analysis helped identify synonyms, abbreviations, and related terms that could be incorporated into the search string. A crucial metric of predictive maintenance, "Remaining Useful Life," was added. Additionally, interchangeable terms related to Prognostics and Health Management, and "prognostics," were also added. Terms related to generative AI such as "generative," "adversarial," "GAN," "VAE," and "variational autoencoder" were included. Finally, aviation, aero, and aircraft were added to ensure the research focused on the aviation sector. challenge_findings.description: Die bereitgestellte SLR identifiziert Datenknappheit bzw. fehlende Run-to-Failure-Daten als Kernproblem und beschreibt die Generierung realistischer synthetischer RUL-/Degradationsdaten als zentrale Rolle generativer KI im Aircraft PdM.

Die SLR benennt Datenaugmentation, Feature Extraction und Missing-Data-Imputation explizit als Primärfunktionen generativer KI und verweist auf GANs, VAEs sowie Hybridformen als die am häufigsten eingesetzten Modellklassen.

Die Evidenz dokumentiert zwar zentrale Umsetzungsbarrieren, quantifiziert aber nicht belastbar, wie weit generative KI in realen MRO-/PdM-Prozessen bereits operationalisiert ist. Für die starke Aussage über einen nur emergenten Industrialisierungsgrad wäre breitere Evidenz zur tatsächlichen Adoption nötig.

Die Schlussfolgerung, dass generative KI vor allem unterstützend und nicht autonom eingesetzt wird, ist plausibel, stützt sich jedoch teilweise auf allgemeine PdM-Barrieren und Eignungsgrenzen statt auf direkte Evidenz zu generativer KI in regulierten Luftfahrtprozessen.

synthesis_output.answer:
## Kurzantwort
Generative KI spielt im Predictive Maintenance für Flugzeuge vor allem die Rolle eines Daten- und Modellierungs-Enablers: Sie kompensiert die in der Luftfahrt besonders ausgeprägte Knappheit an Run-to-Failure-Daten, indem sie synthetische Degradations-, Fehler- und Remaining-Useful-Life-Daten erzeugt und damit genauere Zustandsdiagnosen sowie RUL-Prognosen unterstützt [1][2][3]. Darüber hinaus wird sie methodisch insbesondere für Datenaugmentation, Merkmalsextraktion und die Imputation fehlender Daten eingesetzt; dominierende Modellklassen sind GANs, VAEs und Hybridformen daraus [2][4][5]. In der betrieblichen Umsetzung ist ihre Rolle derzeit eher unterstützend innerhalb bestehender PHM-, PdM- und MRO-Prozesse als autonom ersetzend, weil Interpretierbarkeit, Systemspezifität, Datenverfügbarkeit, Integrationsaufwand und Wissensaustausch weiterhin wesentliche Hürden darstellen [5][6][7].

## Ausgangslage der Flugzeuginstandhaltung
Predictive Maintenance ist in der Luftfahrt strategisch relevant, weil sie auf Basis von Zustands- und Sensordaten potenzielle Ausfälle vor ihrem Eintreten erkennen soll und damit Verfügbarkeit und Kosten beeinflusst [3]. Für die Luftfahrt wird ein erhebliches Potenzial genannt: Ein IATA-Bericht sieht eine mögliche Steigerung der Flugzeugverfügbarkeit um bis zu 35 % durch Predictive Maintenance [3], während für gut ausgelegte PdM-Programme allgemein Kostensenkungen von 30 % berichtet werden und Einsparungen durch Aircraft Health Management und Predictive Maintenance im Milliardenbereich liegen können [8]. Gleichzeitig ist die Datenlage in der Luftfahrt strukturell schwierig: Für viele sicherheitskritische Komponenten existieren nur begrenzt Run-to-Failure-Daten, weil Bauteile aus Sicherheitsgründen vor dem kritischen Versagen ausgetauscht werden [9][1]. Genau an dieser Stelle setzt generative KI an [1][2].

## Kernrolle generativer KI im Aircraft Predictive Maintenance
Die systematische Literaturübersicht identifiziert als primäre Rolle generativer KI im Aircraft PdM die Überwindung von Datenmangel, insbesondere bei seltenen Fehlerfällen und frühen Degradationsstadien [2][4]. Generative Modelle erzeugen synthetische Daten, die reale Run-to-Failure-Verläufe nachbilden, obwohl nur unvollständige oder unausgewogene Realdaten vorliegen [1][9]. Dadurch lassen sich Trainingsdatensätze für nachgelagerte Prognose- und Diagnosemodelle erweitern, was präzisere Vorhersagen des Systemzustands und der Remaining Useful Life ermöglicht [1][2].

Die Literatur formuliert diesen Punkt sehr klar: Das Hauptziel des Einsatzes generativer KI in Aircraft PdM ist die Erzeugung realistischer RUL-Daten, die anschließend für RUL-Prognosen und Fehlerdiagnose genutzt werden [2]. Zugleich wird hervorgehoben, dass generative KI gegenüber traditionellen PdM-Ansätzen besonders dort Vorteile bietet, wo Daten unausgewogen, verrauscht oder lückenhaft sind [2][4]. Damit ist ihre Rolle weniger die eines vollständigen Ersatzes bestehender Prognoseverfahren, sondern die einer vorgelagerten oder integrierten Ermöglichungsschicht für robustere PdM-Modelle [2][5][8].

## Methodische Einsatzfelder
Die Literatur nennt drei Hauptfunktionen generativer KI im Aircraft Health Monitoring und PdM:
- Datenaugmentation durch Generierung zusätzlicher realistischer Beispiele für seltene Fehler- und Degradationsmuster [4][1]
- Merkmalsextraktion bzw. Repräsentationslernen zur Bildung aussagekräftiger Features für Klassifikations- und Prognosemodelle [4][5]
- Imputation fehlender Daten, indem trainierte Modelle fehlende Werte entlang gelernter Datenmuster ergänzen [4]

Diese Funktionen adressieren direkt typische Luftfahrtprobleme: Imbalanced Data, bei der gesunde Betriebszustände die Fehlerdaten deutlich überwiegen, verrauschte Sensordaten und fehlende Messwerte [4][2][6]. Die Übersicht betont, dass generative KI besonders geeignet ist, den Bias datengetriebener Modelle gegenüber der überrepräsentierten Normal-Klasse zu reduzieren, etwa durch Oversampling seltener Fehlerdaten oder durch die Erzeugung synthetischer Anomalien [4].

## Dominierende Modellklassen
Als meistgenutzte Modellklassen werden Generative Adversarial Networks, Variational Autoencoders sowie Kombinationen beider Ansätze genannt [5][2][10]. Die Übersicht berichtet, dass GANs, VAEs und VAE-GAN-Kombinationen die häufigsten generativen Verfahren für die Vorhersage der RUL von Flugzeugkomponenten sind [2][5]. Verschiedene GAN-Varianten wie CGAN, DGAN, WGAN, RNN-GAN oder CR-GAN werden eingesetzt, wobei die Auswahl von Datentyp, Zielproblem und gewünschter Interpretierbarkeit abhängt [6]. VAEs werden insbesondere zur Extraktion informativer Merkmale hervorgehoben [5][6].

## Anwendungsschwerpunkte und Datenbasis
Die untersuchten Arbeiten fokussieren stark auf zustandsbezogene Sensordaten und auf Komponenten mit gut verfügbarer Test- oder Simulationsdatenbasis [6][2]. Besonders dominant ist der Datensatz NASA C-MAPSS, der Informationen zu 100 Turbofan-Triebwerken mit Run-to-Failure-Verläufen enthält und deshalb häufig für RUL-Studien genutzt wird [2][6]. Die Literatur weist aber zugleich darauf hin, dass diese Dominanz eine Verengung des Feldes auf Turbofan-Engines erzeugt und Forschung zu anderen kritischen Flugzeugkomponenten wie Lagern oder Hydrauliksystemen erschwert [2][6].

Neben öffentlichen Datensätzen werden auch Teststände und einzelne reale Betriebsdaten genutzt, etwa zu Hydraulikpumpen, Lagern, APU-Sensordaten eines Airbus A320 oder Flugdatensätzen mit 47 Sensoren pro Flugzeug [11]. Insgesamt deutet dies darauf hin, dass generative KI bereits in mehreren luftfahrtnahen Teilanwendungen erprobt wird, die empirische Basis jedoch komponentenspezifisch und noch nicht breit über die Flugzeugzelle verteilt ist [11][6].

## Einordnung in bestehende PHM- und MRO-Prozesse
Die Quellen sprechen dafür, generative KI als Baustein innerhalb bestehender PHM- und MRO-Architekturen einzuordnen, nicht als losgelösten Ersatz klassischer Instandhaltungslogik [8][7]. PdM selbst ist nicht für alle Systeme geeignet; für manche Systeme sind Ausfälle nicht hinreichend prognostizierbar oder ein regelbasierter bzw. Condition-Based-Ansatz genügt [8]. Außerdem berücksichtigen etablierte Wartungsprogramme und MRB/MSG-Prozesse die Vorteile von PdM bislang nicht systematisch [8].

Vor diesem Hintergrund erscheint generative KI vor allem als Verstärker datengetriebener Teilprozesse: Sie verbessert Fault Diagnosis, unterstützt Entscheidungsfindung, trägt zur Ressourcenoptimierung bei und kann in zentralisierte Plattformen integriert werden, die Sensordaten, Betriebsdaten und Wartungsinformationen zusammenführen [7]. Für MRO-Anbieter wird allgemein empfohlen, digitale Transformation, Predictive Analytics, IoT und KI-Werkzeuge in integrierten Ökosystemen zusammenzuführen [7]. Ein direkter Nachweis, dass generative KI bereits breit produktiv in solchen MRO-Ökosystemen ausgerollt ist, liegt jedoch nicht vor [5][7].

## Reifegrad und Umsetzungsbarrieren
Die praktische Rolle generativer KI ist derzeit durch mehrere Hürden begrenzt:
- geringe Interpretierbarkeit und daraus resultierendes Vertrauensproblem, besonders bei sicherheitskritischen Systemen [6]
- Systemspezifität, weil Modelle, die auf einem Flugzeugsystem trainiert wurden, nicht ohne Weiteres auf andere Systeme übertragbar sind [6]
- hohe Dimensionalität der Flugzeugdaten und teure Echtzeitintegration in bestehende Infrastruktur [6]
- unzureichender Wissensaustausch zwischen Airlines, Herstellern und Forschungseinrichtungen [6]
- starker Fokus auf Sensordaten bei gleichzeitiger Unternutzung textueller und visueller Daten [6]

Die Literatur nennt diese Punkte explizit als zentrale Implementierungsprobleme generativer Modelle im Aircraft PdM [5][6]. Zugleich wird auf positive Entwicklungspfade verwiesen: Kombinationen mit Digital Twins und Sensortechnologien könnten umfassendere Modelle für RUL- und Fehlerprognosen ermöglichen, und mehr Interpretierbarkeit würde die Akzeptanz steigern [12]. Dennoch bleibt der belastbare Nachweis einer breiten operativen Industrialisierung in realen MRO-Prozessen in den vorliegenden Quellen aus [6][7].

## Regulatorisch-sicherheitsnahe Einordnung
Direkte regulatorische Detailquellen zu Zertifizierung oder EASA-spezifischen Anforderungen für generative KI in der Flugzeuginstandhaltung liegen hier nicht vor. Indirekt spricht die Evidenz aber dafür, dass generative KI in sicherheitsnahen Luftfahrtkontexten derzeit eher unterstützend eingesetzt werden sollte: PdM ist nicht universell geeignet [8], Wartungsprogramme sind in etablierte Prozesse eingebettet [8], und die Literatur benennt explizit mangelnde Interpretierbarkeit und Integrationsprobleme als Hürden [6]. Eine belastbare Aussage über konkrete Zulassungs- oder Zertifizierungspfade für generative KI lässt sich daraus jedoch nicht ableiten.

## Schlussfolgerung
Generative KI übernimmt im Predictive Maintenance für Flugzeuge heute vor allem drei Rollen: erstens die Erzeugung synthetischer Daten zur Kompensation fehlender Run-to-Failure- und Fehlerdaten, zweitens die Verbesserung datengetriebener Modelle durch Datenaugmentation, Merkmalsextraktion und Imputation, und drittens die Unterstützung bestehender PHM- und MRO-Prozesse bei Diagnose und Prognose [1][2][4][5]. Ihr größter Nutzen liegt aktuell nicht in autonomer Wartungsentscheidung, sondern in der Verbesserung der Datenbasis und Modellgüte unter den besonderen Randbedingungen der Luftfahrt [1][2][6]. Die methodische Evidenz ist hierfür stark, der Nachweis einer breiten industriellen Operationalisierung dagegen bislang begrenzt [6][7].

Missing information:
- Direkte regulatorische oder normative Quellen zu Zertifizierung, EASA/FAA-Anforderungen oder Compliance-Vorgaben für generative KI in der Flugzeuginstandhaltung fehlen.
- Belastbare Nachweise zum tatsächlichen industriellen Rolloutgrad generativer KI in realen MRO-Organisationen oder Airline-Flotten fehlen.
- Vergleichende Leistungsdaten zwischen generativer KI und nicht-generativen PdM-Verfahren auf einheitlichen luftfahrtspezifischen Benchmarks sind nicht ausreichend belegt.
- Konkrete Fallstudien zu produktiven End-to-End-Implementierungen generativer KI in Flugzeugwartungsplanung, Ersatzteilsteuerung oder Line/Base-Maintenance liegen nicht vor.
